title: Polariton BECs: Theory and Concepts
abstract: Polaritons are a superposition of light and matter, that combine Strong Interferences (of light) with Weak Interactions (of excitons), making them WISI (Weakly-Interacting, Strongly-Interfering) particles. Their condensation is the main highlight of a field which occupies a unique position at the intersection of quantum optics, condensed matter physics and nonlinear dynamics of driven, dissipative systems. This chapter surveys selected theoretical concepts of polariton condensates' formation, coherence and dynamics, with an emphasis on their distinctions from their atomic counterparts and on points of ongoing controversy. We argue that linear and non-interacting effects are undervalued in polariton physics, and that a significant part of the phenomenology -- including bosonic correlations and coherence buildup -- can often be understood without invoking strong interactions or genuine quantum effects.
summary: This survey reviews the theory of polariton condensates, which are hybrid light-matter quasi-particles. It details the theoretical tools needed to describe their many-body physics, contrasting the quantum description with simpler classical analogues. The work highlights that understanding the system requires careful consideration of both linear and non-interacting effects alongside strong correlations.
main_problem: The paper surveys the theoretical concepts governing the formation, coherence, and dynamics of polariton condensates, emphasizing their unique nature compared to atomic BECs.
main_result: The dynamics can be understood by mapping the system to a coherent state evolution, which mathematically mirrors classical coupled harmonic oscillators, suggesting linear effects are often undervalued.
method: The analysis employs a combination of one-particle quantum superposition descriptions (Bloch sphere) and many-body quantum-optical density matrix formalisms.
model_or_system: The system is composed of exciton-polaritons, which are quasi-particles formed by the strong coupling of excitons (matter) and cavity photons (light) in a microcavity. The Hamiltonian describes the coupled light-matter system.
key_observables: Two-polariton reduced density matrix ($ho^{(2)}$), two-photon correlation function ($g^{(2)}(0)$), and coherence measures.
important_parameters: Detuning, effective masses ($m_{ex}, m_C$), and the degree of interaction strength.
main_assumptions: The text argues that while the quantum picture is fundamental, much phenomenology can be explained using semi-classical or non-interacting approximations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the unfolding of light-matter interaction, the two-particle polariton picture, and the two-photon correlation function $g^{(2)}(0)$ showing bunching, antibunching, and no correlation.
paper_structure: The structure moves from defining the polariton quasi-particle (one-particle picture) to describing the many-body quantum state using density matrices, and finally comparing the quantum vs. classical descriptions of the dynamics.
why_interesting: It bridges the gap between standard BEC theory (quantum gases) and quantum optics, providing a rich platform to study non-equilibrium, driven-dissipative many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The one-particle polariton picture. (a) Sketch of a microcavity as two mirrors sandwiching a quantum well, respectively trapping photons and excitons, whose confinement brings in the strong-coupling regime. By the method of images, the cavity mirrors can be removed and the confined polariton becomes propagating again, as originally envisioned by Hopfield in bulk materials. (b) The lower ELP and upper EUP polariton dispersions, arising from the strong coupling of the exciton and photon parabolic dispersions with largely differing masses, so that the exciton appears flat over a broad range of momenta. The strong anharmonicity of the polariton dispersions leads to a plethora of sui...

FIG. 2. The two-particle polariton picture. (a) Polariton blockade with g(2)(0) < 1 as a function of the laser detuning from the lower polariton branch, for three exciton-photon detuning, with the dashed green line at resonance, solid-red at positive cavity detuning (exciton-like LP) and dash-dotted black at negative cavity detuning (photon-like LP). (b) Same as (a) for the negative detuning case and over an enlarged spanning window of laser energy (now shown relative to the exciton), to also include the UP branch, with panel (a) identified in the box ≈−6meV.
